title: userns=keep-id gives "OCI runtime permission denied error"
body:
/kind bug

I am getting this on Arch Linux. Cgroups v2, crun and fuse-overlayfs are in use.

The same command [works fine for a CentOS user](https://www.reddit.com/r/podman/comments/fyyxfs/podman_usernskeepid_getting_error_cannot_setns/fo0cpd1/).

```
podman run --log-level=debug --userns=keep-id -ti --rm busybox sh
DEBU[0000] Using conmon: "/usr/bin/conmon"              
DEBU[0000] Initializing boltdb state at /home/user/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod/bolt_state.db
```

comments.body:
could you share the configuration for your mount points?

What is the output for `cat /proc/self/mountinfo` ?

> could you share the configuration for your mount points?
> 
> What is the output for `cat /proc/self/mountinfo` ?

A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

@mestaritonttu Are you still seeing this issue?
@giuseppe Any ideas?
Yep, still seeing this with podman 1.9.3 and an up-to-date system.

it seems something specific to Arch, I've never seen it before on Fedora or Ubuntu.

I'd have to try directly on Arch.  @mestaritonttu how have you installed podman, crun and fuse-overlayfs?

`sudo pacman -S podman crun fuse-overlayfs`
For v2 cgroups: https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/Cgroups#Disabling_v1_cgroups

thanks.  I've installed arch, switched to cgroup v2, configured a new user (with additional subuids) and this is what I get:

I see you are using `distribution: '"antergos"'`.  It seems discontinued (https://web.archive.org/web/20190522031004/https://antergos.com/blog/antergos-linux-project-ends/), how are you keeping it updated?

Thanks for taking the time to test. So it is some user error that is impossible for me to figure out :(
Antergos was only the original installation method, their repo is gone and this is just normal Arch now.

Edit: just tested with a newer Arch installation and indeed I do not get the problem.

Hi, I just solved this same issue but the problem and solution are not documented anywhere as far as I know and should probably be addressed somehow.

After a lot of trial and error and noticing that I didn't have any issue with a fresh user even though I already erased `~/.local/share/containers` and `~/.config/containers` I somehow found out that the permissions on my home directory where preventing the command from working as expected.

My previous home directory permissions:
```
drwx------ 132 mtorromeo root 12288 Sep 28 20:36 /home/mtorromeo
```

I can change it in 2 ways to make it work.
Assigning my primary group to the directory (without even having to change the group permissions):
```
drwx------ 132 mtorromeo mtorromeo 12288 Sep 28 20:36 /home/mtorromeo
```

or giving access to `other` like this (obviously not recommended):
```
drwx-----x 132 mtorromeo root 12288 Sep 28 20:36 /home/mtorromeo
```

using another group like `users` (which I am part of) and giving it access to the directory doesn't work:
```
drwxr-x--- 132 mtorromeo users 12288 Sep 28 20:36 /home/mtorromeo
```

I think this behavior is extremely odd and should be documented somehow, maybe making so that podman prints out a more useful error message, because if you happen to have this problem it can make you go crazy...

@mtorromeo, thanks a lot. I stumbled into the same problem. For me the solution was slightly different.

I found that I had to create a private group for my user (which didn't exist before) and chgrp the directories `$HOME/.local` **and** `$HOME/.local/share` to this group. It's quite weird, because neither directory had any group rights assigned, and `$HOME/.local/share/containers` was a symlink to elsewhere:

```
martin@artemis:~> ls -ald /home $HOME $HOME/.local $HOME/.local/share $HOME/.local/share/containers /mnt/git/$USER/ /mnt/git/$USER/containers
drwxr-xr-x   9 root   root      94 May  7  2020 /home
drwxr-xr-x 113 martin martin 20480 Feb 12 12:25 /home/martin
drwx------   5 martin martin  4096 Dec 26  2018 /home/martin/.local
drwx------  56 martin martin  8192 Feb 12 12:16 /home/martin/.local/share
lrwxrwxrwx   1 martin martin    26 Feb 12 12:16 /home/martin/.local/share/containers -> /mnt/git/martin/containers
drwxr-xr-x  19 martin martin  4096 Feb 12 12:01 /mnt/git/martin/
drwxr-xr-x   4 martin martin    32 Feb 12 11:44 /mnt/git/martin/containers
```

If I chgrp either `$HOME/.local` **and** `$HOME/.local/share` e.g. to `users`, I can't start containers with `podman run --userns=keep-id ...` any more. The group assignments for other directories shown above don't seem to matter.

I have the same issue on Ubuntu 20.04. I have graphroot overridden because I need it to be on another drive, but it and its parent directory have correct permissions (my user and its private group), and my home directory too.
